guest_role: Founder and CEO of Hims & Hers (just gone public via SPAC); co-founder of Atomic
assertiveness_why: Harry asks well-prepared, curious questions and occasionally injects a light challenge (e.g. on the IPO 'pop'), but the conversation is close and friendly, with virtually no direct contesting of Andrew's claims.
question_style: Open, generous questions clearly informed by conversations with mutual friends/investors named beforehand (Jules Molts, Alex Bard, Jack Abraham); one notable moment where he pushes on whether skipping the IPO 'pop' concerns Andrew, but otherwise receives answers without friction.
tone: Very personal and matey — the two are established friends ('from dinner in London to now recording this show'), high energy, playful asides (Snoop Dogg story, baby jokes), minimal formality.
depth: Goes fairly deep into operational and financial mechanics: SPAC vs. traditional IPO trade-offs, pricing efficiency and the cost of a first-day 'pop', margin structure of a pharma subscription business, and time-management/specialization-vs-generalist team-building philosophy.
harry_quotes: Do you worry about not having the pop... is that a concern?
How do you think about your relationship to money and how's that changed over time?
How did you get Snoop Dogg involved so early on?
learnings: Hims & Hers deliberately moved from generalist to specialist hiring almost immediately after reaching product-market fit, rather than waiting, because specialization compounds value once there's a proven thing worth selling.

A first-day IPO 'pop' is framed as evidence of mispricing and unnecessary founder dilution rather than a genuine signal of success, which is why the SPAC's front-loaded pricing with committed public investors appealed to the company.
Deliberately compressing planning language from quarters/weeks down to same-day windows (before breakfast, after lunch, post-caffeination) meaningfully increases how much a team actually executes.
Requiring every hire to be better at their specific job than the hiring manager keeps talent density rising as the company scales, even though it's psychologically uncomfortable for junior managers.

The company's healthy ~76% margins and 90% recurring revenue stem from selling pharmaceutical subscriptions with long lifetime retention, giving room to balance growth and profitability rather than choosing one.
repeat_guest_callback: For those that missed our first episode, tell me, how did you make your way into the world of startups?
notable: Recorded literally the week Hims & Hers went public via SPAC, with the guest speaking as a newly public CEO about the process in near-real time.
